responses.strategic_rationale: The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Standards Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework will create pressure on Denmark to adopt platform-neutral standards, making supplier-neutral design harder to exclude. This may lead to a more resilient digital identity system.
options: Lead negotiations with EU bodies to include platform-neutrality requirements in Danish digital identity planning
Develop and publish open standards for fallback authentication that reduce dependence on Apple or Google as trust anchors
Build relationships with key stakeholders within the European Digital Identity Wallet framework to influence procurement decisions
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between platform-neutrality and existing infrastructure, but the options overlook potential pushback from incumbent operators.

levers.name: Procurement Intervention
levers.consequences: Influencing procurement processes around AltID will create a demand for platform-neutral solutions, making it more difficult for suppliers to lock-in users. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop and publish procurement language that requires Danish digital identity systems to include platform-neutral or certified fallback access paths
Work with procurement stakeholders to ensure AltID requirements include platform-neutrality and fallback capabilities
Engage with suppliers to develop platform-neutral solutions that can be integrated into existing infrastructure
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between procurement influence and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential resistance from incumbent operators.

levers.name: Demonstrator Development
levers.consequences: Developing non-production technical demonstrators will provide credible technical evidence that platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models are feasible. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator that authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards
Explore a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether
Focus on developing a single, feature-rich demonstrator that showcases platform-neutral and fallback capabilities
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between technical demonstration and procurement influence, but the options overlook potential limitations in demonstrating platform-neutrality.

levers.name: Regulatory Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging with regulatory bodies will create a demand for platform-neutral solutions, making it more difficult for suppliers to lock-in users. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Work with regulators to develop and publish new regulations that require Danish digital identity systems to include platform-neutral or certified fallback access paths
Engage with regulatory bodies to ensure existing regulations are enforced and do not hinder platform-neutrality efforts
Develop relationships with key stakeholders within the regulatory community to influence procurement decisions
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between regulation and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential resistance from incumbent operators.

levers.name: Coalition Building
levers.consequences: Building a coalition of civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee will create pressure on Denmark to adopt platform-neutral standards. This may lead to a more resilient digital identity system.
options: Develop relationships with key stakeholders within the coalition to influence procurement decisions
Work with coalition partners to develop and publish open standards for fallback authentication that reduce dependence on Apple or Google as trust anchors
Engage with coalition members to ensure existing regulations are enforced and do not hinder platform-neutrality efforts
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between coalition building and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential limitations in demonstrating platform-neutrality.

levers.name: Resilience Framing
levers.consequences: Framing the issue as a resilience problem will create pressure on Denmark to adopt platform-neutral standards, making supplier-neutral design harder to exclude. This may lead to a more resilient digital identity system.
options: Develop and publish reports that highlight the risks of supplier concentration and promote platform-neutrality
Engage with stakeholders to ensure existing regulations are enforced and do not hinder platform-neutrality efforts
Work with regulators to develop and publish new regulations that require Danish digital identity systems to include platform-neutral or certified fallback access paths
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between resilience framing and supplier concentration risk, but the options overlook potential resistance from incumbent operators.

levers.name: Technical Research
levers.consequences: Conducting technical research will provide credible evidence that platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models are feasible. This may lead to a reduction in supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a detailed report on the feasibility of platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models
Explore new technologies and standards that can be used to reduce dependence on Apple or Google as trust anchors
Focus on developing a single, feature-rich demonstrator that showcases platform-neutral and fallback capabilities
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between technical research and procurement influence, but the options overlook potential limitations in demonstrating platform-neutrality.

responses.summary: Add 'engage with suppliers to develop platform-neutral solutions that can be integrated into existing infrastructure' to Procurement Intervention.

levers.name: Forklift Strategy
levers.consequences: Implementing a forklift strategy would directly affect the project's ability to adapt to changing requirements, potentially leading to costly rework and delays. This, in turn, could compromise the project's overall timeline and budget.
options: Develop a robust and iterative approach to requirements gathering, ensuring that changes are incorporated seamlessly into the project plan.
Establish a clear and transparent change management process, enabling stakeholders to provide input and feedback throughout the project lifecycle.
Implement a flexible and adaptive project structure, allowing for easy reconfiguration in response to changing requirements or priorities.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between rigidity and flexibility, but the options overlook potential scalability issues with iterative approaches.

levers.name: Stakeholder Engagement
levers.consequences: Effective stakeholder engagement is crucial for the project's success, as it can influence the adoption of platform-neutral solutions and secure buy-in from key stakeholders. Inadequate engagement may lead to resistance or even opposition from influential stakeholders.
options: Develop a comprehensive stakeholder analysis, identifying key influencers and their interests in order to tailor engagement strategies accordingly.
Establish a transparent and inclusive communication plan, ensuring that all stakeholders are informed and involved throughout the project lifecycle.
Create a dedicated stakeholder management team, responsible for building relationships and addressing concerns in real-time.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between information sharing and control, but the options overlook potential power dynamics with influential stakeholders.

levers.name: Digital Identity Standards
levers.consequences: Developing standards for digital identity is critical to ensuring interoperability and security across different platforms. Inadequate or inconsistent standards may lead to fragmentation and reduced confidence in the digital identity infrastructure.
options: Establish a clear and comprehensive set of standards, aligned with international best practices and guidelines from organizations such as eIDAS and NSIS.
Develop a modular and flexible standards framework, allowing for easy adaptation and extension in response to changing requirements or emerging technologies.
Create a dedicated standards committee, responsible for reviewing and updating the standards in real-time, in collaboration with relevant stakeholders.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between standardization and flexibility, but the options overlook potential trade-offs between security and usability.

levers.name: Procurement Framework
levers.consequences: Establishing a procurement framework that prioritizes platform-neutrality is essential for ensuring the long-term sustainability of the digital identity infrastructure. Inadequate or biased frameworks may lead to continued dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
options: Develop a transparent and inclusive procurement process, ensuring that all stakeholders are involved in the selection of vendors and technologies.
Establish clear criteria for vendor evaluation, prioritizing factors such as platform-neutrality, security, and interoperability.
Create a dedicated procurement team, responsible for managing the procurement process and ensuring compliance with established standards and guidelines.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between openness and control, but the options overlook potential conflicts of interest with influential vendors.

levers.name: Cybersecurity Posture
levers.consequences: Maintaining a strong cybersecurity posture is critical to ensuring the integrity and confidentiality of digital identity data. Inadequate or complacent approaches may lead to security breaches and compromised trust in the infrastructure.
options: Develop a robust and proactive cybersecurity strategy, incorporating best practices from organizations such as NIST and ISO.
Establish clear incident response procedures, ensuring that all stakeholders are prepared to respond quickly and effectively in the event of a security breach.
Create a dedicated cybersecurity team, responsible for monitoring and addressing potential threats in real-time.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between security and convenience, but the options overlook potential trade-offs between risk tolerance and compliance requirements.

levers.name: Partnership Strategy
levers.consequences: Building strategic partnerships is essential for ensuring the long-term sustainability of the digital identity infrastructure. Inadequate or ineffective partnerships may lead to continued dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
options: Develop a clear and comprehensive partnership strategy, aligned with international best practices and guidelines from organizations such as eIDAS and NSIS.
Establish clear criteria for partner evaluation, prioritizing factors such as platform-neutrality, security, and interoperability.
Create a dedicated partnership team, responsible for managing partnerships and ensuring compliance with established standards and guidelines.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between openness and control, but the options overlook potential conflicts of interest with influential partners.

levers.name: Innovation Ecosystem
levers.consequences: Fostering an innovation ecosystem is critical to ensuring the continued relevance and effectiveness of the digital identity infrastructure. Inadequate or stagnant ecosystems may lead to reduced confidence in the infrastructure.
options: Develop a clear and comprehensive innovation strategy, aligned with international best practices and guidelines from organizations such as eIDAS and NSIS.
Establish clear criteria for innovation evaluation, prioritizing factors such as platform-neutrality, security, and interoperability.
Create a dedicated innovation team, responsible for managing the innovation process and ensuring compliance with established standards and guidelines.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between stability and change, but the options overlook potential trade-offs between risk tolerance and regulatory requirements.

responses.summary: Add 'establish a dedicated cybersecurity team' to Cybersecurity Posture.

levers.name: Platform-Independent Governance Framework
levers.consequences: Establishing a platform-independent governance framework would directly affect the way Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is managed and regulated, potentially leading to more autonomy and control for the country. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of coordination and standardization among different stakeholders.
options: Implement a decentralized governance model with rotating leadership
Establish a permanent government agency responsible for overseeing digital identity matters
Create an industry-led consortium to develop and enforce standards
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between centralized control and distributed autonomy, but the options overlook potential conflicts of interest and accountability mechanisms.

levers.name: Digital Identity Standards Harmonization
levers.consequences: Harmonizing digital identity standards would have a direct impact on the interoperability and compatibility of different systems, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new barriers for innovative solutions that don't conform to standardized approaches.
options: Develop a set of open standards for digital identity verification
Create a certification program for vendors who meet specific standards
Establish a reference implementation for digital identity protocols
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between standardization and innovation, but the options overlook potential lock-in effects.

levers.name: Public-Private Partnership Model
levers.consequences: Developing a public-private partnership model would have a direct impact on the way Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is funded and maintained, potentially leading to increased investment and expertise. However, this might also create new conflicts of interest and accountability challenges.
options: Create a joint venture between government agencies and private companies
Establish a public-private funding mechanism for digital identity initiatives
Develop a co-creation model where stakeholders collaborate on solution development
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between public and private interests, but the options overlook potential power imbalances.

levers.name: Digital Identity Skills Development
levers.consequences: Investing in digital identity skills development would have a direct impact on the capacity and expertise of Denmark's workforce, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of adapting to changing technologies and requirements.
options: Develop training programs for government employees and private sector workers
Create a certification program for digital identity professionals
Establish an industry-led skills development initiative
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between workforce capacity and technological change, but the options overlook potential skill obsolescence.

levers.name: Digital Identity Data Governance
levers.consequences: Developing a data governance framework for digital identity would have a direct impact on the way personal data is collected, stored, and used, potentially leading to increased trust and reduced risks. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of balancing individual rights with national security concerns.
options: Establish a data protection authority responsible for overseeing digital identity data
Create a data sharing framework between government agencies and private companies
Develop guidelines for responsible data practices in digital identity
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between personal data protection and national security, but the options overlook potential trade-offs.

levers.name: Digital Identity Procurement Reform
levers.consequences: Reforming Denmark's procurement process for digital identity solutions would have a direct impact on the way government agencies acquire and implement new technologies, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of adapting to changing requirements and supplier relationships.
options: Streamline the procurement process through electronic tendering
Create a centralized procurement agency for digital identity solutions
Develop a co-creation model where stakeholders collaborate on solution development
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between efficiency and adaptability, but the options overlook potential supplier resistance.

levers.name: Digital Identity Awareness Campaign
levers.consequences: Launching an awareness campaign for digital identity would have a direct impact on public understanding and engagement with digital identity issues, potentially leading to increased trust and reduced risks. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of balancing information dissemination with potential misinformation.
options: Develop educational materials for schools and community programs
Create a public awareness campaign through social media and advertising
Establish an industry-led initiative to promote digital identity literacy
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between public awareness and misinformation, but the options overlook potential information overload.

levers.name: Digital Identity Research Initiative
levers.consequences: Investing in research for digital identity would have a direct impact on the development of new solutions and technologies, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of adapting to changing requirements and integrating research findings into practice.
options: Establish a research institute focused on digital identity
Create a collaborative research program with industry partners
Develop guidelines for responsible innovation in digital identity
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between research and practical application, but the options overlook potential knowledge silos.

levers.name: Digital Identity Standards Enforcement
levers.consequences: Enforcing standards for digital identity would have a direct impact on the way solutions are developed and implemented, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of balancing enforcement with innovation and adaptability.
options: Establish a standards authority responsible for enforcing digital identity standards
Create a certification program for vendors who meet specific standards
Develop guidelines for responsible standards development
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between standardization and innovation, but the options overlook potential lock-in effects.

levers.name: Digital Identity Skills Certification
levers.consequences: Certifying digital identity skills would have a direct impact on the capacity and expertise of Denmark's workforce, potentially leading to increased efficiency and reduced costs. However, this might also create new challenges in terms of adapting to changing technologies and requirements.
options: Develop certification programs for government employees and private sector workers
Create a certification program for digital identity professionals
Establish an industry-led skills development initiative
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between workforce capacity and technological change, but the options overlook potential skill obsolescence.

levers.consequences: Developing a data sharing framework for digital identity would have a direct impact on the way personal data is collected, stored, and used, potentially leading to increased trust and reduced risks. options: Establish a data protection authority responsible for overseeing digital identity data
Create a data sharing framework between government agencies and private companies
Develop guidelines for responsible data practices in digital identity

options: Establish a research institute focused on digital identity

Create a certification program for vendors who meet specific standards
Develop guidelines for responsible standards development

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Example: "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

2026-Mar-08

Project start ASAP

This plan requires one or more physical locations. It cannot be executed digitally.

**Explanation:** This project, while focused on digital infrastructure, *heavily relies* on physical elements. It requires a physical base in Copenhagen for research, development, and stakeholder meetings. The project involves building demonstrators, engaging with government bodies, and influencing procurement processes, all of which necessitate physical presence and interaction. The project also requires a physical location with access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings.
